title: Pregnancy and herbal medicine
subtitle: How to think about safety in the first, second, and third trimester
excerpt: A foundations article on using herbal preparations during pregnancy, which herbs are widely accepted, which require caution, which to avoid, and how to think about 'natural means safe'. Includes a practical guide to the pregnancy safety categories used across this section.
sourceNotes: EMA HMPC monographs for individual herbs, pregnancy safety classifications drawn from individual monographs. WHO Traditional Medicine Strategy 2014, 2023, safety of traditional medicine in special populations. Romm A., Botanical Medicine for Women's Health (2010), clinical herbalism pregnancy safety framework. Mills S. and Bone K., The Essential Guide to Herbal Safety (2005), detailed herb-specific safety classifications.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb that traditionally stimulates or increases menstrual flow. Many emmenagogues are classified with caution in pregnancy because of potential effects on uterine tone.
A herb or preparation that stimulates uterine contractions. Uterotonics are contraindicated in pregnancy (apart from at term under obstetric supervision).

attrs.title: Not medical advice
attrs.body: This article is an educational overview of how to think about herbal safety in pregnancy. It is not a substitute for advice from your midwife, GP, or a qualified herbalist who knows your individual circumstances. When in doubt, ask.

content.text: Pregnancy is the context in which the 'natural means safe' assumption does most damage. Many herbs that are entirely safe for a non-pregnant adult carry genuine concerns during pregnancy, not because they are toxic in the conventional sense, but because they act on the uterus, on fetal development pathways, or on hormone systems in ways that matter when another person is growing. This article explains how to read the safety notes in this section, what the key categories of concern are, and which herbs commonly appear in home kitchens with guidance on use.

are herbs that stimulate menstrual flow. Historically, this category was important in the domestic herbal tradition because 'provoking the terms' (inducing menstruation) was a coded reference to terminating an early pregnancy. Many of the herbs in this section, fennel, mugwort, pennyroyal, blue cohosh, carry emmenagogue classifications in the historical literature. The concern is primarily at therapeutic doses (multiple cups a day of a strong preparation), not at culinary use (a fennel-seed dressing on salad, a sprig of thyme in a sauce). This section notes pregnancy-caution flags where traditional emmenagogue classifications apply, but distinguishes between culinary use (acceptable) and therapeutic-dose repeated preparations (discuss with midwife).

, herbs that stimulate uterine contractions, are the higher-concern category. Blue cohosh, pennyroyal, tansy, and savin (juniper berry at high dose) are the most cited uterotonics in the historical literature and are not included in this section. Raspberry leaf is the uterotonic most commonly encountered in home use, it is widely used in the third trimester specifically to tone the uterus for labour, and is generally considered safe from 32 weeks onwards, but should not be used in the first and second trimester. This section does not include raspberry leaf preparations precisely because the trimester-specificity of its use requires more individual guidance than a recipe card can reliably provide.

Most herbs have never been systematically studied for safety in human pregnancy, clinical trials in pregnant populations are, for obvious reasons, rare. The absence of evidence of harm is not the same as evidence of absence of harm. For herbs in this category, the guidance in this section follows traditional practice and the precautionary principle: herbs with long records of use in pregnancy without documented adverse effects (nettle, ginger at low dose, chamomile at culinary strength) are flagged as acceptable with appropriate caution; herbs without such a track record are flagged as 'discuss with midwife'.
Herbs generally accepted in pregnancy at low doses

The following herbs appear in this section and are widely considered acceptable in pregnancy at low, culinary-to-tea-strength doses: ginger (up to 1 g dried equivalent per day for morning sickness, WHO and EMA documentation exists), chamomile (one or two cups of standard infusion, not daily strong therapeutic preparations), nettle leaf (nutritive tonic use throughout pregnancy is traditional and well-regarded), elderflower infusion (standard tea strength), lavender bath and infusion (normal concentrations). In all cases, the qualifier is 'at culinary-to-tea-strength, not at high therapeutic dose'. Multiple cups of a strong preparation daily is a different matter from one occasional cup.
Herbs to discuss with a midwife during pregnancy

Fennel (weak oestrogenic activity, emmenagogue classification at therapeutic dose), St John's Wort (not studied in pregnancy; drug interactions), valerian tincture (sedative strength, alcohol content), echinacea (immune stimulant, limited pregnancy data). These are not absolute contraindications in all cases, but they are herbs where individual circumstances matter enough that the answer depends on the person, not just the herb.
The first trimester is the most cautious period

Organ formation occurs in the first 12 weeks. This is the period when even herbs with good safety records in later pregnancy are approached with extra caution by traditional herbalists. The second and third trimesters are generally more permissive for herbs with established safety records, the risk profile changes as the baby is no longer undergoing organogenesis. This does not mean anything goes in the second trimester, but it does explain why some safety notes in this section say 'first trimester caution' rather than a blanket prohibition.

Your midwife is the first point of contact for any question about a specific herb during pregnancy. A qualified medical herbalist (MNIMH or MCPP registered in the UK) can give more detailed individual advice. The Herbal Safety website (maintained by the National Institute of Medical Herbalists) has current safety summaries for commonly used herbs. For anything beyond a single occasional cup of a well-established herb, the answer is: ask someone who knows both herbalism and your specific pregnancy.
